The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the prayer at the march — "Rise up, LORD, and let thine enemies be scattered; and let them that hate thee flee before thee": whenever the ark set forward, Moses prayed that the LORD would arise and scatter his enemies; the witnesses note the significance — the prayer that God himself, present in the ark, would go before and scatter all who opposed his people; the lesson — that the cause of God's people is God's own cause, their enemies his enemies (the appeal to God to arise and act, to scatter those who hate him, the confidence that when God arises none can stand); the wider use — this prayer is taken up by David as the opening of the great psalm of God's triumphant march, "Let God arise, let his enemies be scattered: let them also that hate him flee before him," Psalm 68:1; and applied to the ascension and triumph of Christ, who, having risen, scatters his enemies and leads captivity captive, Psalm 68:18; Ephesians 4:8; the figure — the triumphant advance of the presence of God; the confidence of the church on the march, that God arises and his enemies are scattered; the figure of the victorious progress of Christ and his people against all opposition. The figure of God arising to scatter his foes as his people go forth.

On "when the ark set forward, that Moses said," the grammatical-historical Clarke and witnesses note the occasion. Clarke and Gill note the prayer offered whenever the ark set forward (the march begun with prayer) — the occasion (the prayer at the going forth).

On "Rise up, LORD, and let thine enemies be scattered; and let them that hate thee flee before thee," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the prayer. Calvin and Gill note the appeal for God to arise and scatter his enemies (the cause of God's people God's own, Psalm 68:1) — the figure (Christ risen scattering his foes, Ephesians 4:8); the providence (the prayer at the going forth, the prayer at the rest to follow, vv.36). The verse displays Moses's prayer at the going forth of the ark.

The witnesses agree the substance: whenever the ark set forward, Moses prayed that God would arise and scatter his enemies (Calvin, Clarke, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the prayer for God to arise: Moses's prayer — the appeal for God himself, present in the ark, to go before and scatter all who oppose his people (their enemies his own), taken up by David in the great psalm of God's triumph (Psalm 68:1) and applied to the risen Christ who scatters his foes (Psalm 68:18; Ephesians 4:8), the confidence of the church on the march. The verse displays the arising-of-God crux. Moses's prayer at the going forth of the ark displays the crux: Rise up, LORD, and let thine enemies be scattered (Psalm 68:1, 18; Ephesians 4:8) — the prayer for God to arise and scatter all who oppose his people, taken up in the psalm of God's triumph and applied to the risen Christ who scatters his foes, the confidence of the church on the march. The verse displays Moses's prayer at the resting of the ark (v.36).
36:
"And when it rested, he said, Return, O LORD, unto the many thousands of Israel." The very brief verse notes Moses's prayer at the resting of the ark, closing the chapter. The grammatical voices note the prayer. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "when it rested, he said, Return, O LORD, unto the many thousands of Israel" — whenever the ark rested, Moses prayed that the LORD would return and abide with the multitudes of Israel; the when it rested (the ark stopping, the camp at rest); the Return, O LORD, unto the many thousands of Israel (the prayer for God to abide with his people in the rest, to dwell among the multitudes); the figure (the prayer for God's presence in the rest as in the march, that he abide with his people; the two prayers together — God arising to scatter the foe in the march, and returning to dwell with his people in the rest; the figure of God's presence both leading in the way and dwelling in the rest, "the LORD shall preserve thy going out and thy coming in," Psalm 121:8; the desire for God to dwell among the many thousands of his people, the presence of God the joy of the rest; the figure of Christ dwelling with his people, his presence the blessing of both the journey and the rest; the prayer that God would not leave but abide with the great congregation, the church the dwelling of God).

On "when it rested, he said," the witnesses note the rest. Gill notes the prayer at the ark's resting (the camp at rest) — the occasion (the prayer at the rest).

On "Return, O LORD, unto the many thousands of Israel," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the prayer: "Return, O LORD, unto the many thousands of Israel" — the prayer for God to abide with the multitudes (God's presence in the rest, Psalm 121:8); the figure (God dwelling with his people, the presence the joy of the rest); the providence (the prayer at the rest, closing the chapter). The verse displays Moses's prayer at the resting of the ark.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the prayer — "when it rested, he said, Return, O LORD, unto the many thousands of Israel," the prayer (Keil, Gill); "when it rested, he said," the rest (Gill); "Return, O LORD, unto the many thousands of Israel," the prayer (Psalm 121:8, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the prayer. The verse marks the ark: at rest. The verse displays the LORD: returning. The verse displays the thousands: of Israel. The verse displays the presence: abiding. The prayer displays God dwelling with his people in the rest. The verse displays the rest: blessed. The verse displays the presence: abiding. The verse displays the people: God's dwelling. Moses's prayer at the resting of the ark displays the presence of God in the rest: Return, O LORD, unto the many thousands of Israel (Psalm 121:8) — the prayer for God to abide with the multitudes of his people, his presence the blessing of the rest as of the march, a figure of Christ dwelling with his people. The verse closes the setting forth from Sinai, before the murmuring at Taberah (ch.11).
